ai_industry.title: People Hate AI Even More Than They Hate ICE, Poll Finds
ai_industry.title_cht: 最新民調：美國民眾對 AI 的反感程度超越移民執法局
ai_industry.title_ja: 世論調査：AIへの反感は移民局ICEを上回る――米国の民意を読む
summary_points_cht: NBC 民調顯示，46% 受訪者對 AI 持負面態度，僅 26% 正面，比 ICE 移民執法局更不受歡迎
即便如此，承認使用 AI 工具的受訪者從 2025 年 12 月的 48% 上升至 3 月的 56%，認知與行為出現矛盾
57% 受訪者認為 AI 風險大於收益，AI 成為 2026 年中期選舉的核心議題
AI 涉及監控、失業、能源消耗、軍事等爭議，科技公司正砸重金影響選舉走向
summary_points_ja: NBC世論調査でAIへの否定的意見が46%、肯定が26%にとどまり、移民局ICEより不人気という結果に
矛盾も顕著：57%が「リスクがメリットを上回る」と答える一方、AI利用率は12月の48%から56%に上昇
AI監視・失業・エネルギー問題・軍事利用への批判が重なり、反感が高まっている
AI規制は2026年中間選挙の主要争点となり、テック大手は親AI候補への支持を強めている
ai_industry.content_cht:
根據 NBC 最新民調，人工智慧在美國公眾眼中的形象跌至歷史低點。調查顯示，46% 的登記選民對 AI 持負面態度，僅 26% 表示好感，27% 中立——負面程度甚至超越了長期爭議的移民執法局（ICE）。唯一比 AI 更不受歡迎的議題，是伊朗和民主黨。

這份數據之所以值得關注，在於它揭示了一種深刻的認知矛盾：承認使用 ChatGPT 等 AI 平台的受訪者比例，從 2025 年 12 月的 48% 上升至今年 3 月的 56%。換句話說，人們一邊繼續使用 AI，一邊對它抱有強烈的警惕甚至反感。

公眾不滿的來源是多方面的：AI 被認為威脅白領就業、加劇 AI 上癮問題、協助政府監控，以及在戰場上提高殺傷效率。Palantir 為 ICE 大規模驅逐移民行動提供 AI 支援、OpenAI 向五角大廈授權技術，都進一步激化了民眾情緒。能源方面，美國正在以前所未有的規模興建數據中心，導致周邊社區面臨水資源短缺和電費飆升，2025 年數據中心項目取消數量較前年翻了四倍。

AI 監管已成為 2026 年美國中期選舉的核心戰場，大型科技公司正投入鉅額資金支持對 AI 友善的候選人。
ai_industry.content_ja:
NBCの最新世論調査によると、人工知能（AI）は米国の登録有権者の間で深刻なイメージ危機に直面している。46%がAIに否定的な感情を持ち、肯定的なのは26%にとどまる。移民・関税執行局（ICE）よりも不人気という結果となり、AIより嫌われているのはイランと民主党だけという状況だ。

注目すべきは、この認識と行動の矛盾だ。「AIのリスクはメリットを上回る」と答えた人が57%に達する一方、ChatGPTなどのAIプラットフォームを使用していると認めた人は12月の48%から56%に増加している。人々はAIへの不信感を抱えながら、使い続けているのだ。

反感の源泉は多岐にわたる。ホワイトカラーの雇用喪失への懸念、AI依存問題、監視への加担、そして戦場での殺傷力強化などが挙げられる。また、エネルギー問題も深刻で、データセンターの急増による水不足や電気代高騰への住民の抵抗から、2025年にはデータセンター計画のキャンセルが4倍に増えた。

AI規制は2026年中間選挙の最重要テーマとなり、テック大手は親AI候補への支持に多額の資金を投じている。
ai_industry.content_original:
People really hate artificial intelligence, according to the latest NBC poll.Â
NBC surveyed a group of registered voters and found that the concept of artificial intelligence was so unpopular that 46% of respondents said they hold negative feelings towards the concept, and only 26% reported positive connotations, while 27% were neutral.
Even the Immigration and Customs Enforcement agency, which has been the subject of nationwide protests spanning months, was viewed more favorably, as was President Donald Trump, whose current net approval rating is -19.Â In fact, the only topics viewed more negatively than AI were Iran and the Democratic Party.
The risks that come with artificial intelligence proliferation have been a major topic of public discussion for the past year as the technology further entrenches itself in modern society.
Experts have argued that widespread AI adoption in the business world will bring about a white-collar unemployment catastrophe. Some say AI is already impacting the labor market negatively, with a clear hit suffered by early-career workers in vulnerable sectors, even though the actual returns on productivity are still highly contested.
AI addiction has quickly become a phrase we are all too familiar with, despite repeated warnings against the mental health concerns that come with an overreliance on chatbots and numerous studies linking increased reliance on AI with less cognitive ability. AI chatbots have also been repeatedly accused of inciting violence and promoting self-harm, and numerous grieving families are suing major chatbot providers like OpenAI and Character.AI.
The complicity of AI companies in widely unpopular government actions worldwide has also contributed to the bad reputation AI is building for itself. AI has turbocharged surveillance efforts and increased fatality in war zones around the world. Calls for a boycott have increased as Palantir signed agreements to supply the AI technology aiding the massive anti-immigrant crackdown led by ICE, and OpenAI has stood firmly behind its controversial decision to lease its technology to the Pentagon.
Also attracting controversy is the intense energy demand posed by increased AI usage. The United States is in the midst of an unprecedented data center buildout, but the locals living near these centers have repeatedly complained about water shortages, rising utility bills, and worsening air quality. People are so fed up that data center project cancellations quadrupled in 2025 due to local resistance. In response, a growing number of states and other localities have begun to consider implementing moratoriums on new AI data centers.
But there is some cognitive dissonance among the general public. Fifty-seven percent of the respondents to the survey said that the risks of AI outweigh the benefits. Despite that, the respondents who admit to using AI platforms like ChatGPT rose from 48% in December 2025 to 56% in March.
The poll comes as artificial intelligence has become a major battleground in the upcoming midterm elections. Despite the considerable real estate that AI holds in political discourse, the technology is still incredibly underregulated, and a lot of the major names in the industry would like it to stay that way. Big tech is spending exorbitant amounts of money to back politically aligned candidates and undermine the AI hawks running for election in November 2026, while said candidates are making AI a core issue in their campaigns.
Even though the Trump-led Republican Party has been very protective of the AI industry and sees it as crucial to national security, AI critics and champions have come from both sides of the aisle. In the NBC poll, the majority of voters surveyed said that neither party is adequately dealing with the threat of artificial intelligence.

ai_industry.title: Meta's New AI Model Is Reportedly Delayed Again. Is 'Avocado' Toast?
ai_industry.title_cht: Meta 新一代 AI 模型「Avocado」再度延遲，被指推理與編碼能力落後對手
ai_industry.title_ja: MetaのAIモデル「Avocado」が再延期、推論・コーディングで他社に後れ
summary_points_cht: Meta 旗艦新模型 Avocado 最早要到 5 月才會發布，在推理、編碼、寫作方面內部測試落後 Google、OpenAI、Anthropic
Meta 今年 AI 投入預算從 720 億美元上調至 1,150-1,350 億美元，但大量資金未能換來預期成果
Meta 以 143 億美元收購 Alexandr Wang 旗下新創股份，任命其為首席 AI 官，押注快速追趕
Meta 官方稱模型「雖非最佳，但能展示快速進步的軌跡」，預告仍將在年內持續推出新模型
summary_points_ja: 次世代モデル「Avocado」は5月以降に延期、推論・コーディング・ライティングの内部テストでGoogle/OpenAI/Anthropicに劣後
Metaは今年のAI投資を720億ドルから最大1,350億ドルに引き上げたが、成果は期待に届かず
Alexandr WangのAI企業に143億ドルを投資し最高AI責任者に任命、急追を目指す
Meta広報：「良いモデルを出す。重要なのは急速な軌跡を示すこと」と述べ、年内の継続リリースを予告
ai_industry.content_cht:
據《紐約時報》報導，Meta 的新一代旗艦 AI 基礎模型「Avocado」再度延期，最早可能到 5 月才會與外界見面。根據消息人士透露，Avocado 在推理、編碼和寫作等關鍵能力上的內部測試成績，仍落後於 Google、OpenAI 和 Anthropic 的最新模型。

Meta 近年在 AI 領域的投資力度驚人。今年 1 月的財報電話會議中，公司宣布將 AI 支出從去年的 720 億美元大幅上調至 1,150-1,350 億美元。此外，Meta 以 143 億美元取得 Alexandr Wang 創辦的新創企業股份，並任命其為首席 AI 官，試圖加速追趕競爭對手的步伐。

然而大量資本投入目前尚未轉化為模型能力的領先優勢。Google 於去年 11 月推出 Gemini 3 後，在多項基準測試中超越對手，而 Meta 的模型迭代速度相對較慢。

Meta 發言人在聲明中表示，下一個模型雖然未必是最強的，「但更重要的是展示我們正在快速前進的軌跡，並將在年內持續推出新模型」。目前 Avocado 的正式發布時程仍未確定。
ai_industry.content_ja:
ニューヨーク・タイムズの報道によると、Metaの次世代基盤AIモデル「Avocado」が再び延期となり、最も早くても5月のリリースとなる見込みだ。複数の関係者によれば、Avocadoは推論、コーディング、ライティングの内部テストでGoogleやOpenAI、Anthropicの最新モデルに及ばないとのことだ。

MetaのAI投資は規格外の規模に達している。1月の決算説明会でMetaは今年のAI支出を昨年の720億ドルから最大1,350億ドルへ引き上げると発表。さらにAlexandr WangのAIスタートアップに143億ドルを投じ、最高AI責任者（CAO）に任命するなど、業界トップを追いかけるための大型投資を続けている。しかし潤沢な資金はまだ期待通りの成果を生み出していない。

Meta広報担当者はCNETに対し「次のモデルは優れたものになるが、より重要なのは我々が急速に進歩している軌跡を示すこと。年内も新モデルを継続的にリリースしていく」とコメントした。
ai_industry.content_original:
Meta's Avocado isn't ripe quite yet. The company has reportedly delayed the release of its next-generation foundational model until May, according to The New York Times, citing unnamed sources. The model has fallen short "on internal tests for reasoning, coding and writing," compared with rival models from Google, OpenAI and Anthropic.
Meta has spent billions of dollars overhauling its efforts to build artificial intelligence models and products, including purchasing a stake in Alexandr Wang's startup for $14.3 billion to make him chief AI officer. The company has poured buckets of cash into hiring top AI engineers last year across its organization. In a January earnings call, Meta confirmed it plans to raise its spending from $72 billion last year to $115 billion to $135 billion, attributing the increase to supporting its AI labs. But all that money hasn't bought the company the results it hoped for, with Google, OpenAI and Anthropic consistently lapping Meta with their newer models.
A Meta spokesperson told CNET, "As we've said publicly, our next model will be good, but more importantly, show the rapid trajectory we're on, and then we'll steadily push the frontier over the course of the year as we continue to release new models. We're excited for people to see what we've been cooking very soon."
That rapid trajectory is key to catch up and keep pace with other AI builders. Google leapfrogged its rivals in November with its Gemini 3 model, showcasing its impressive coding and research abilities. OpenAI was quick to follow with updates to GPT-5. More recently, Claude Code and Cowork from Anthropic have proven to be the most reliable agentic AI available, tools that can handle tasks without human babysitting. However, the biggest AI news from Meta this year is that it's buying Moltbook, a social media platform designed exclusively for AI bots.
Meta has been in the news for other reasons. A renewed wave of privacy concerns has crested over people using smart glasses, particularly the Meta Ray-Ban glasses, to record others without their knowledge or consent. A lawsuit alleges that human staffers behind Meta's smart glasses reviewed footage of people who clearly didn't know they were being recorded, like while they were undressing or having sex. On the social media side of the business, a high-profile trial is debating whether platforms like Meta's Instagram and Facebook are addictive to teens and pose significant health risks.

ai_industry.title: Oracle Is Walking Away From Expanding Its Stargate Data Center With Oracle
ai_industry.title_cht: OpenAI 放棄擴建與甲骨文合作的 Stargate 德州資料中心
ai_industry.title_ja: OpenAIがOracleとのStargate拡張を断念、次世代Nvidiaチップ確保を優先
summary_points_cht: OpenAI 決定不再擴建位於德州 Abilene、與 Oracle 合作的 Stargate 資料中心，轉而尋求其他夥伴
現有 Abilene 站點計劃使用 Nvidia Blackwell 晶片，但電力供應至少一年後才能上線
OpenAI 希望提前在更大叢集取得 Nvidia 下一代晶片，因此不再加碼現有合作
Oracle 否認報道，但僅表示現有項目進展順利，對擴建計畫的問題保持沉默
summary_points_ja: OpenAIがテキサス州AbildeneのOracle主導Stargateデータセンター拡張を断念、他パートナーを模索
既存サイトはNvidiaBlackwellチップ予定だが電力供給まであと約1年かかる見込み
その頃には別の場所で次世代Nvidiaチップを大規模クラスターで確保したいOpenAIの戦略的判断
OracleはX上で報道を否定したが既存プロジェクトの順調さを述べるのみで拡張計画には言及せず
ai_industry.content_cht:
據 CNBC 報導，OpenAI 已決定放棄擴建其與 Oracle 合作、位於德州 Abilene 的 Stargate AI 資料中心。知情人士透露，Abilene 現有站點預計採用 Nvidia Blackwell 處理器，但電力供應最快也要一年後才能到位。屆時，OpenAI 希望已能在其他地點透過更大規模的叢集，優先獲得 Nvidia 下一代晶片，因此不願繼續在此合作上加碼投入。

Oracle 在 X 上否認相關報道「完全不實」，並強調現有項目按計畫推進，但未回應擴建計畫的問題。此事折射出 Stargate 計畫快速推進中出現的合作裂痕：Oracle 已在 Abilene 砸下數十億美元，卻面臨合作夥伴的戰略轉向。AI 基礎設施領域的合作格局正在悄然生變。
ai_industry.content_ja:
CNBCの報道によると、OpenAIはOracleと共同でテキサス州Abileneに建設中のStargate AIデータセンターの拡張計画を断念した。関係者によれば、AbildeneサイトはNvidiaのBlackwellプロセッサを予定しているが完全な電力供給まで約1年かかる。その頃には別の場所でNvidiaの次世代チップをより大規模なクラスターで確保したいOpenAIは、Abilene拡張への追加投資に消極的だ。

OracleはXで報道を「虚偽」と強く否定し既存プロジェクトの順調さを主張したが、拡張計画については言及しなかった。AI基盤をめぐるOpenAIとOracleの協力体制に亀裂が生じたことが明らかになった。
ai_industry.content_original:
OpenAI Is Walking Away From Expanding Its Stargate Data Center With Oracle (cnbc.com) 40
The current Abilene site is expected to use Nvidia's Blackwell processors, and the power isn't projected to come online for a year. By then, OpenAI is hoping to have expanded access to Nvidia's next-generation chips in bigger clusters elsewhere, said the person, who asked not to be named due to confidentiality. In a post on X, Oracle called the reports "false and incorrect." However, it only said existing projects are on track and didn't address expansion plans.
CNBC notes: "Oracle secured the site, ordered the hardware, and spent billions of dollars on construction and staff, with the expectation of going bigger."

ai_industry.title: I spent 17 hours at a legal conference. These were the two questions haunting lawyers about AI.
ai_industry.title_cht: 法律周大會 17 小時親歷：律師們面對 AI 的兩大核心焦慮
ai_industry.title_ja: 法律カンファレンス17時間潜入：弁護士たちのAIへの2つの核心的不安
summary_points_cht: LegalWeek 大會 AI 主導所有議題，但律師最常問的是：「怎麼讓律師真的用起來？」
兩大焦慮：工作安全感（科技公司已因 AI 效率裁員）以及嚴重不足的內部培訓
Harvey 與 Legora 等法律 AI 新創押注數十億美元，等待法律行業加速採用生成式 AI
客戶要更快更便宜、投資人要回報，雙重壓力迫使保守的法律業開始正視 AI 轉型
summary_points_ja: LegalWeekはAI一色、しかし会場で最も多かった質問は「どうすれば弁護士に使わせられるか」という根本的なもの
2大不安：雇用喪失（AtlassianやBlockがAI効率化で人員削減済み）と社内トレーニング不足
HarveyやLegoraなどが数十億ドルを賭けて法律業界のAI普及加速を待っている
クライアントの速さ・低コスト要求と投資家リターン要求が、保守的な法律業界を変革へ押し出しつつある
ai_industry.content_cht:
今年 LegalWeek 在紐約 Javits 中心舉辦，AI 幾乎佔據了所有展台和面板討論。記者花 17 小時在場內外觀察，卻發現最常聽到的問題不是「AI 有多強」，而是一個令人唏噓的基礎問題：「怎麼讓律師真的用起來？」

這背後有兩層焦慮。第一是工作安全感：Atlassian 和 Block 等科技公司已明確將裁員與 AI 效率提升掛鉤，法律圈對此心知肚明。第二是培訓缺口——買了工具卻沒有系統使用指引，大量 AI 投資停留在試用而非落地。

Harvey（估值 110 億美元）和 Legora（估值逾 50 億美元）等法律 AI 新創的商業模式，全押在律師能更快使用 AI 上。客戶要求更快、更便宜的法律服務；投資人要求數十億美元注資換來回報。這雙重壓力，正把天性審慎的法律行業推向一個它習慣迴避的問題：如何真正轉型。
ai_industry.content_ja:
ニューヨークのJavitsセンターで開催されたLegalWeekでは、AIがほぼすべてのブースとパネルを席巻した。17時間会場を歩き回った記者が最も多く耳にしたのは「AIがいかに優れているか」ではなく、ある種の悲哀を感じさせる問い──「どうすれば弁護士に実際に使わせられるか」──だった。

この問いの背後には2層の不安がある。第1層は雇用の安全性。AtlassianやBlockがAI効率化を理由に人員削減を実施しており、法律業界もひとごとではない。第2層はトレーニング不足。ツールを導入しても使い方の体系的な指導がなく、多くのAI投資が試用段階にとどまっている。

HarveyとLegoraなどのリーガルAIスタートアップは数十億ドルを賭けて法律業界のAI採用加速を待つ。クライアントは速さと低コストを求め、投資家はリターンを要求する。この二重圧力が、変化を避けてきた法律業界を転換の岐路へと追い込んでいる。
ai_industry.content_original:
- At Legalweek, AI was a hot topic, but many lawyers remained skeptical about its implementation.
- Concerns over job security and inadequate training hindered their adoption of legal-tech tools.
- Billions of dollars are riding on lawyers moving faster with tools from startups Harvey and Legora.
At Legalweek this week, lawyers got a mix of shmooze, swag, and one topic on repeat: artificial intelligence. It headlined nearly every panel and powered every booth pitch. Still, the question I heard most — in hallways, at happy hours, even from the people selling the tools — was disarmingly basic: How do we get lawyers to use it at all?
What's happening in Big Law looks increasingly like what's already unfolding in tech. Companies like Atlassian and Block have already tied recent layoffs to AI-driven efficiency gains, signaling that the technology's promise of higher productivity can also mean fewer jobs.
Billions of dollars are now riding on the assumption that AI will do the same for professional services. But inside law firms, adoption is a mixed bag, even as clients demand faster, cheaper work and investors expect returns on the billions of capital flooding into legal tech.
Legalweek, held at New York City's Javits Center, has become an annual progress report on whether generative artificial intelligence is actually changing an industry that's cautious by design. It was my second year attending, and the demos were noticeably slicker. AI "agents" were everywhere across the expo hall, sold as digital coworkers that can draft, review, and run multi-step workflows that used to require a junior associate and a lot of coffee.
But the conference also produced a quieter signal that the adoption curve isn't matching the hype. When Microsoft's Steven Abrahams, who leads product work on Copilot integrations, asked a room of attendees to raise their hands if they use software to automate contract review — one of the clearest use cases for large language models — only a few hands went up.
Onstage, the warnings were blunt: Clients will take their business elsewhere if firms don't change their habits. "Revenue is at risk," Emma Dowden, Burges Salmon's chief operating officer, said during a panel hosted by Harvey, an $8 billion startup selling software to law firms and corporate legal teams.
In that same session, a moderator asked Robert Clark, an in-house lawyer at the brand agency Dentsu, whether "AI maturity" will factor into how companies choose outside counsel a year from now. "I'm judging today," he said, adding that it's "cringeworthy" to hear firms say they hired a new chief innovation officer while balking at buying licenses for legal artificial intelligence platforms.
All week long, lawyers tried to explain the tepid response. Dowden pointed to fear. Lawyers, she said onstage, worry about what automation means for their own jobs, how it could eat into work billed by the hour, and whether they understand the technology well enough to defend it to a skeptical client. The anxiety can curdle into resistance. Partners may want the tech's upside, she suggested, as long as another practice area in the firm tests it first.
People often assume younger lawyers will be the easiest converts. Not always, said Sarah Eagen, who leads learning and development at the megafirm Cleary Gottlieb, during a panel. Cleary Gottlieb has rolled out Legora, a Harvey competitor, firmwide. Still, many associates see automation as a threat, Eagen said, just as they've spent years and money buying into a career built on entry-level work.
Lawyers aren't alone in this anxiety, as Business Insider's Tim Paradis has written. In industries where AI adoption is further along, job cuts are routinely being framed as the natural next step — the cost of doing business in an era where work moves faster, and companies insist they simply need fewer people to do it.
Lawyers are trained to make decisions only after they've gathered the facts. Speakers suggested that when firms actually invest in training, lawyers are more likely to use the tools — because they understand the guardrails.
Ian Nelson, who runs Hotshot, a company that helps law firms build training programs, said during a panel that too few firms offer any AI training at all. "There seems to be this mentality" that training can wait until a firm has licensed a tool, he said. That's short-sighted, Nelson argued, because some lawyers will use chatbot tools anyway. And when training does happen, he said, it's often too narrow. Think tool-specific demos without the context about risks and the firm's own policies.
Legalweek's loudest question was still the one making vendors squeamish: Why won't lawyers use the tools? But as the week went on, a second question started to surface.
If AI really can produce better, more cost-effective service, at what point does resistance start to look like malpractice?
Corporate lawyer Michael Pierson raised the issue during Harvey's panel. Just over two years ago, Pierson and his fellow former FisherBroyles partner Joel Ferdinand broke from the firm to launch Pierson Ferdinand, a smaller, distributed outfit that leans heavily on tools like Harvey and runs without any associates.
"If we're not using AI in the daily delivery of our legal services to our clients, is it malpractice per se? I don't know," he said. "But we're in the business of client service, and anything that gets us to excellent work product, we should be exploring."
Correction: March 14, 2026 — An earlier version of this story misattributed a quote from Harvey's panel. The speaker was Robert Clark.

ai_industry.title: 2 legal-tech rivals' biggest fight might not be against each other
ai_industry.title_cht: 法律 AI 雙雄 Harvey 與 Legora：最大競爭對手可能不是彼此
ai_industry.title_ja: 法律AIライバルのHarveyとLegora：最大の競合はお互いではないかもしれない
summary_points_cht: Harvey（估值 110 億美元）與 Legora（估值逾 50 億美元）是法律 AI 頭兩號玩家，但戰略路徑迥異
Legora 融資 5.5 億美元並首度收購加拿大法律科技新創 Walter，加速追趕
Harvey 聯手 LegalTech Fund 展開生態系投資，試圖以廣度鞏固護城河
兩家公司真正的共同敵人是整個法律行業的保守慣性與偏低的 AI 採用率
summary_points_ja: Harvey（評価額110億ドル）とLegora（50億ドル超）は法律AIの2大プレーヤー、しかし戦略は対照的
Legoraは5.5億ドルを調達しカナダのリーガルテックWalterを初買収、猛追態勢へ
HarveyはLegalTech Fundと連携して若いリーガルテックへ投資開始、エコシステム戦略で堀を強化
両社の真の共通敵は法律業界全体の保守的な慣性とAI低採用率だというのが業界の分析
ai_industry.content_cht:
法律 AI 有兩個名字無人不曉：Harvey 和 Legora。Harvey 估值約 110 億美元，已服務全美超過半數最大律所；Legora 估值逾 50 億美元，剛完成 5.5 億美元新一輪融資並首度收購加拿大法律科技新創 Walter。

兩家公司都相信 AI 將顛覆企業法律服務，但路徑截然不同：Legora 以追趕者姿態快速擴張，透過收購加速垂直整合；Harvey 則向投資方向延伸，聯手 LegalTech Fund 佈局生態系投資，試圖以更廣泛的版圖鞏固護城河。

分析指出，Harvey 和 Legora 的最大競爭對手未必是彼此。兩家共同面臨的核心挑戰，是整個法律行業的保守慣性。傳統律所文化天生審慎，說服資深合夥人改變工作方式遠比說服科技投資人更難。法律 AI 的真正決戰，是在律師的辦公桌前。
ai_industry.content_ja:
リーガルAIの世界でHarveyとLegoraの2つの名前は誰もが知っている。Harveyは評価額約110億ドルで米国最大の法律事務所の半数以上が顧客、Legoraは評価額50億ドル超で直近の5.5億ドル調達とカナダのリーガルテックWalterの初買収を完了した。

両社ともAIが企業法務を変えると確信するが戦略は対照的だ。Legoraは買収で垂直統合を加速する追撃者、HarveyはLegalTech Fundとのエコシステム投資で堀を広げる守備者の姿勢を取る。

業界分析では、HarveyとLegoraの最大の競合相手はお互いではないと指摘する。両社が共に直面するのは法律業界全体の保守的な慣性と低いAI採用率だ。伝統的な法律事務所ではベテランパートナーに変化を納得させることが最大の壁となっている。
ai_industry.content_original:
- This post originally appeared in the Business Insider Today newsletter.
- You can sign up for Business Insider's daily newsletter here.
I've got a brief on some legal beef.
Two AI startups are duking it out over the future of corporate law. Harvey and Legora both agree that artificial intelligence will upend the legal world. But they have very different views on who'll do it.
BI's Melia Russell, our resident legal-tech wizard, profiled Legora and its 26-year-old founder, Max Junestrand.
Legora is unquestionably the underdog. Its recent valuation of more than $5 billion is nothing to sneeze at, but it's still well below Harvey's reported $11 billion. A potential price tag only tells part of the story, but Harvey also counts more than half of the largest US law firms among its customers. Legora has cracked only about 20% of that group.
Legora's second-place status isn't slowing it down. Melia was first to report that Legora used its new $550 million funding round to secure its first acquisition, Canadian legal-tech startup Walter. (I'm not sure why legal tech loves using first names for their companies. I'll check with Melia on that one.)
Not to be outdone, Harvey is partnering with The LegalTech Fund to start investing in young legal-tech startups.
It's a sign of the times, as Melia has previously reported. Legal-tech giants looking to add more features and specialities to their products are hunting for deals.
Legora and Harvey's biggest fight might not ultimately be with each other.
There are still traditional legal players like Thomson Reuters and RELX. And while they've certainly taken some bumps this year, they still hold an incredible amount of proprietary data that gives them a strong edge (and staying power).
But Legora, Harvey, and other AI-powered legal-tech startups face an even bigger potential challenge with their own customers.
The efficiencies they're looking to provide could upend the current business of some of the people they're looking to sell to. I realize being too good at your job sounds like a made-up problem, but the risk is there.
While in-house lawyers love speeding up their workflows, Big Law works on billable hours. There's a fine line between AI streamlining how they work and cutting into how much they can charge clients.
Even if you think those fears are overblown, Big Law also needs to consider the impact the tech has on young lawyers getting experience. David Goldschmidt, a longtime partner at high-profile firm Skadden, told Melia he's worried smarter machines don't necessarily make better lawyers.

ai_industry.title: Social media giants have grand plans for AI. Creators fear they'll be left out.
ai_industry.title_cht: 社群平台的 AI 大夢，卻讓創作者感到被遺棄
ai_industry.title_ja: ソーシャルメディア大手のAI野望、クリエイターは「置き去り」を恐れる
summary_points_cht: Instagram 測試「Shop the look」功能，在未告知創作者的情況下以其名義推銷商品，被批「像偷竊」
Meta、TikTok、Pinterest 等平台正大力押注 AI，創作者感覺已成次要考量
科技公司以 AI 替代人工創作，創作者憂心自身內容被用來訓練 AI 卻未獲補償
分析指出大科技公司有機會讓創作者共享 AI 利潤，但目前的做法顯示並不打算這樣做
summary_points_ja: Instagramが「Shop the look」機能をテスト、クリエイターに無断で商品をプロモーション、「盗みのようだ」との批判
Meta・TikTok・Pinterestなど各プラットフォームがAIに傾倒、クリエイターは二の次にされた感覚
テック企業がAIでクリエイターの仕事を代替、コンテンツがAIトレーニングに使われても報酬なしの懸念
大手テック企業にはクリエイターをAIの恩恵に参加させる機会があるが、現在の行動はその意思を示していない
ai_industry.content_cht:
社群媒體巨頭們正大力轉向 AI，創作者卻感到自己越來越像一個被遺忘的配角。

最近的引爆點來自 Instagram。Meta 測試了一個名為「Shop the look」的功能，在未告知創作者、更未支付佣金的情況下，利用他們的貼文推銷商品。超過百萬粉絲的時尚 Influencer Julia Berolzheimer 表示：「感覺就像偷竊，他們在用我的臉和名字賣東西，我卻毫不知情。」Meta 稱這只是「限量測試」，強調 Meta 本身不從中抽成。

Instagram 的事件只是冰山一角。TikTok 和 Pinterest 也在推行類似的 AI 變現功能，創作者的內容正被平台用來訓練 AI、推動廣告精準化，卻幾乎不獲回報。

分析人士指出，大科技公司有能力設計出讓創作者共享 AI 收益的機制——但目前的行動方向，顯示他們並不打算這樣做。這場 AI 變現的盛宴，平台是主角，創作者只是食材。
ai_industry.content_ja:
ソーシャルメディアの巨人たちが一斉にAIへ舵を切る中、クリエイターたちは自分たちが「置き去り」にされていると感じている。

最近の発火点はInstagramだ。Metaは「Shop the look」機能をテストし、クリエイターに無断でコミッションも支払わずに彼らの投稿を使って商品をプロモーションした。100万人以上のフォロワーを持つインフルエンサーのJulia Berolzheimerは「盗みのようだ。自分の顔と名前で何かを売っているのに私は何も知らなかった」と批判した。Metaは「限定テスト」と説明しMeta自身はコミッションを取らないと述べた。

TikTokやPinterestも同様のAI収益化機能を推進しており、プラットフォーム全体でクリエイターのコンテンツがAIトレーニングや広告ターゲティングに活用されているが恩恵はほぼ還元されていない。

大手テック企業にはクリエイターがAIの利益を分かち合える仕組みを設計する力があるが、現在の行動はその意思を示していない。AIによる収益化という宴で、プラットフォームが主役でありクリエイターは食材に過ぎない。
ai_industry.content_original:
- Influencers are reckoning with a new boogeyman: artificial intelligence.
- Social apps seem infatuated with AI, and it's making some creators feel like an afterthought.
- Big Tech has an opportunity to help creators share in AI's profits — if they choose to do so.
The days of special treatment for influencers may be numbered as big social apps turn their attention to a shiny new object: artificial intelligence.
The tension recently surfaced on Instagram. The platform angered some creators by testing a feature that used AI to tag and sell products using their posts without giving them a heads-up (or a commission). The tool, called "Shop the look," was a stark reminder from Meta: you make the content, but we own the digital real estate.
"It felt like stealing," said Julia Berolzheimer, a fashion and lifestyle influencer with over one million Instagram followers. "To have my name and my face and my likeness be pushing something without my consent or even my knowledge."
A Meta spokesperson told Business Insider the feature was a "limited test intended to help people explore products that match their interests." They added that Meta itself does not earn a commission, and that it's exploring "various changes" as it continues to test the feature and gather feedback.
Instagram's shopping test — and similar features on TikTok and Pinterest — have added to broader creator concerns about how AI could affect their careers. Recent AI experiments, such as Meta's influencer AI chatbots and TikTok's AI avatar tool for marketers, have hinted at a world in which creators lose some work to AI.
The AI revolution doesn't need to be a death knell for the creator economy. If Instagram were to offer influencers a commission on its "Shop the look" sales, it could become a passive income source for creators, for example. There's ample opportunity for Big Tech to help creators share in the profits of AI — if they decide to.
Jack Conte, the CEO of the creator-monetization platform Patreon, recently told Business Insider he's not anti-AI.
"I think the tech is really cool, and I think it's going to help humans make really beautiful things," he said. "But that doesn't give people carte blanche to roll it out in a way that just creates a bloodbath for the world's creative people."
Creators are losing some leverage
Influencers are accustomed to special treatment, whether that's gaining VIP entry to events or receiving brand freebies in the mail.
That elevated status was particularly evident in the early 2020s when there was a mad dash among social apps to woo influencers with billion-dollar creator funds and other splashy rewards. Competition for talent was fierce after TikTok showed up in the US and threatened to carve out market share.
While social media platforms previously went all in on the creator economy and supporting emerging creators, "now it doesn't feel like that," said Bethany Everett-Ratcliffe, a fashion creator with about 53,000 followers who was in Instagram's now-shuttered test of an affiliate program.
"A lot of these platforms are starting to think about their creators like Uber thinks about their drivers," Conte said. "They want to help them for now, until they don't need them anymore."
Big Tech is so focused on AI that creators can feel like they aren't a priority.
Meta mentioned AI or artificial intelligence over 100 times in its 2025 annual report, while creators came up six times. In January, the company said it would reinvest a lot of its revenue into "very attractive investment opportunities in AI infrastructure and talent." The company recently bought Moltbook, a social network for AI agents.
As the gaze of the social media giants turns elsewhere, creators have less leverage.
There's also an endless on-ramp of new creators if social apps annoy their current rosters. There are now about 1.5 million full-time content creators, a 750% increase from 2020, according to a 2025 Interactive Advertising Bureau estimate. AI is making content creation easier, spurring a wave of new influencers vying for attention.
"I think your average creator is going to lose a lot of value due to what AI can recreate," said Lindsey Gamble, a VP at the influencer-marketing platform Izea.
"There's this limitless amount of content where you don't necessarily need humans to push out the content," he said.
Big Tech could write creators into its AI playbook
Can the Big Tech companies steamroll ahead without creators? Maybe.
Meta and OpenAI created AI-only social feeds in the past year (that have received mixed reactions). With AI-generated content, users will have ample things to scroll past and, therefore, space to view ads.
However, will that content be good enough to entice people to stay? And will brands view AI feeds as less premium?
"In an advertising environment, you actually need trust because you need that ad, whether it's sitting on an image or adjacent to an image, to be something that a consumer feels like is real," said Amber Venz Box, president of the influencer-affiliate platform LTK.
Influencers bring that human touch — and trust — to these apps.
YouTube is already making hard choices that weigh the benefits of AI against its risk to content quality. The company recently cracked down on a slew of popular AI channels that it had deemed "spam," for example. Meanwhile, social startups have sprung up over the past year that explicitly reject AI, such as a revamped version of Vine called diVine.
Social media companies that go too far with AI could set themselves up for an apology tour to win back the trust of creators and their fans. The tricky task ahead for social platforms is embracing a new technology without uprooting the livelihoods of the creators who help sustain their massive businesses.
A recent Patreon survey found that 67% of creators felt "somewhat or very negative about the impact of AI on their world overall."
"While AI can make content creation faster and more efficient, the value of a creator has always been the authentic connection they've built with their audience," said Hannah Hamn, a talent manager at Parker Management.

ai_industry.title: AI Could Change Homebuying. How Listings and Negotiations Are Already Shifting
ai_industry.title_cht: AI 如何改變購房體驗：虛擬看房、智慧議價已成真
ai_industry.title_ja: AIが住宅購入を変える：バーチャル内見とAI交渉支援はすでに現実に
summary_points_cht: Collov AI 等工具可將房源照片轉為虛擬佈置、3D 導覽和室內設計預覽，幫助買家想像未來家居
越來越多買家用 AI 生成的市場分析數據輔助議價，但專家警告 AI 資訊存在錯誤率
AI 可從超過 300 個品牌自動推薦家具，並從照片生成互動式影片看屋體驗
房地產專家建議：AI 適合情境模擬和靈感啟發，重大財務決策仍需專業顧問把關
summary_points_ja: Collov AIなどのツールで物件写真をバーチャルステージング・3Dツアー・インテリア提案に変換できる
AI生成の市場分析データを交渉に活用するバイヤーが増加、ただし専門家はAI情報の誤りに警告
300以上のブランドから自動で家具をレコメンド、静止画からインタラクティブなウォークスルー動画を生成
不動産専門家：AIはシミュレーションとアイデア出しに有効だが重要な財務判断はプロへの相談が必要
ai_industry.content_cht:
在競爭激烈的房地產市場，AI 工具正悄然改變買家與賣家的互動方式。CNET 記者實測了 Collov AI 這款專為房產仲介設計的平台，它能將房源照片轉化為虛擬佈置效果、生成互動式 3D 看屋影片，並提供來自超過 300 個品牌的家具搭配推薦，幫助買家在未正式入住前就能預覽心目中的居家樣貌。

議價環節也正在悄悄變化。越來越多買家開始將 AI 生成的市場分析報告帶入與仲介和銀行的談判桌，試圖用數據強化談判籌碼。然而受訪專家提醒，AI 工具在房地產領域的資訊準確率仍不穩定，容易出現數據錯誤或遺漏本地市場細節。

結論是：AI 非常適合用於情境模擬和激發靈感，卻還不足以支撐重大財務決策。它是有力的輔助工具，但購房這種人生大事，仍需要有專業顧問在旁把關。
ai_industry.content_ja:
競争の激しい不動産市場において、AIツールは静かに買い手と売り手の関係を変えつつある。CNETの記者は不動産エージェント向けのCollov AIを試した。このツールは物件写真をバーチャルステージングに変換しインタラクティブな3Dウォークスルー動画を生成し、300以上のブランドから家具コーディネートを提案できる。

交渉の場面も変化している。AI生成の市場分析データを交渉材料にする買い手が増えているが、専門家はAIツールの不動産情報の精度はまだ不安定でデータエラーや地域詳細の見落としがあると警告する。

結論：AIはシミュレーションとアイデア出しには非常に有効だが重要な財務判断を支えるには不十分だ。住宅購入という人生の一大決断には人間の専門家のサポートが欠かせない。
ai_industry.content_original:
With the housing market as hot as it's been, aspiring buyers can turn to new technology to help navigate their search.
I've used ChatGPT to help redesign my bedroom, and while it was a fun process, I wouldn't base any substantial design or financial decisions on it. It was finicky, made errors and included inaccuracies.
I came across Collov AI, a tool that's designed to help realtors transform their online listings and close deals faster. It offers virtual staging, 3D tours and interior design technology. I wanted to look at it from the buyer's perspective to see if it could help there, too.
Plus, I spoke to an expert about how homebuying negotiations are already changing, with buyers using AI-generated information to guide their talks with realtors and banks -- and whether that approach is effective or too error-prone to be useful.
AI can help you visualize a future home
With Collov AI, you can browse images from online listings or take photos during showings and upload them to the platform to stage a space, adapt or remove elements and even get furniture recommendations from more than 300 brands. You can also visualize different interior designs and style suggestions without spending thousands of dollars on professional renderings.
You can also take an AI-powered virtual tour by uploading static photos of a space to generate an interactive video walkthrough. (It doesn't require a floor plan to create the tour; it's driven by the sequence of photos a listing agent provides, which helps prevent the AI from randomly arranging rooms.)
These features can also help renters visualize a space in their own style, especially if they're traveling or a partner or housemate can't join them for a viewing.
Payton Stiewe, president of real estate brokerage Engel & Völkers in San Francisco, Marin County and Burlingame, uses Collov AI to show buyers how a property can look when it's furnished in their preferred style or even empty, with the Furniture Removal tool.
"When we tour homes with buyers, one of the biggest challenges isn't the house itself. It's what's inside it," Stiewe explains. "Even when a home is professionally staged, or especially when sellers still have their personal belongings in the space, buyers can struggle to see past someone else's style."
Using Collov AI, you can see what your dream home would look like in your style in seconds.
"If they're having trouble picturing a room, I take a photo and instantly show them what that space would look like in their style," Stiewe says. "Instead of asking them to imagine it, I show them. That clarity changes everything. They stop second-guessing and start making confident, informed decisions."
Collov AI built its own diffusion model from the start because, while other AI interior design tools exist, many don't comply with regulations from the Multiple Listing Service and the National Association of Realtors that prohibit AI-generated photos from altering the structure of a space, Markk Tong, Collov AI's founding marketing director, says.
I noticed this happening when I was "designing" in ChatGPT. It kept changing the structure of the room -- moving windows and doorways and even expanding the space.
"You cannot alter the window, the door or the architecture elements of your original photo," Tong says. "You can, however, transition interiors and exteriors from night to day, summer to fall, rain to shine, declutter a desk, fill a pool or remove a car from the driveway."
That's the goal, anyway. Here's how it worked in practice.
How to transform a space based on photos you see in a property listing
If you want to try it out, too, go through the Collov AI sign-up steps and pick your plan. Membership ranges from $19 to $127 per month. The AI virtual staging and interior design features are available on the $19/month model, but if you want the AI virtual tour, you'll need the $79/month tier. There is a free trial available, however.
I tested out the AI Virtual Staging feature. This is the interface:
I perused property listings and picked a house I liked that was well-priced but needed work. I really liked the bones of this house, but its styling was lackluster.
I downloaded six photos of different rooms from the listing to transform in Collov AI.
After I uploaded the first photo, it detected the furniture, which I could manually erase if I didn't like it.
Next, I selected the room type and my preferred design style from the preset templates. I picked the Midcentury look.
Within seconds, it generated five styles, like this:
In one design, it added a piece of furniture and a little wall behind it in the house's open archway in an AI-fueled hallucination. I used the chat feature to flag it.
While it removed it, it then added an extra cabinet in the kitchen. So it had already changed the structure of the house.
I tried another image. This time, I picked the Farmhouse design.
Here's the original photo versus Collov AI's rendering :
I was surprised to see one version completely change the room's structure, though -- something Collov AI is not supposed to do.
I asked Tong about this. He said the typical causes include input photo constraints, room geometry ambiguity and model stochasticity -- aka the inherent randomness of AI tools. Collov AI tries to mitigate this by generating multiple variations so users can choose the most accurate one, along with unlimited free regenerations.
I also wanted Collov AI to help with backyard layouts, so I chose the "Outdoor" room type with a Farmhouse look. The AI Design Agent feature also includes a "Landscape Design" option.
Here's the before and after:
And here's the "Modern" rendition. I picked one style but asked it to add the fairy lights from another version. Collov automatically changed the photo to night to highlight the lights.
It was a lot of fun seeing spaces instantly redesigned in a range of different styles. While I didn't use all of Collov AI's features, it was helpful to visualize what a space might look like in another style, especially since many listing photos are lackluster.
But you do still have to watch out for AI weirdnesses because, sometimes, it'll stick a wall where there isn't one or change the layout of the room.
Can AI help with home-buying negotiations?
I also spoke with Cain Cooke, chair of the Real Estate Institute of South Australia (REISA), who is an expert voice on the Industry Advisory Board for the Australian Institute of Business.
He's seen a major shift in the past 18 months in how buyers are arriving to first conversations with agents far better informed and engaged.
"Buyers are using AI to prepare for negotiation by prompting it to identify comparable sales, understand seller motivation signals, and even script out negotiation approaches before they pick up the phone to an agent," Cooke says. "The research phase has compressed dramatically, but more than that, the preparation phase has too."
But he warns buyers may arrive at the table overconfident if they're relying on an AI-generated strategy they believe shows the full picture -- especially if the AI's research contains errors.
"AI is a great starting point, not a finishing point," he says.
In the vein of tools like Collov AI, which could eventually be built into realtor websites, Cooke says AI may be better used in the real estate market to help sellers create conversational, intelligent interfaces for live listings instead of stagnant pages like those on Zillow, making it easier for buyers to spot the home that might work best for them.
He predicts we'll see more automated property valuations, AI rental management and faster settlements.
"On automated valuations, we're already seeing AI-driven tools move well beyond the more blunt median-price estimates of a few years ago," Cooke says. "The next generation will layer in hyperlocal variables, school zone changes, infrastructure announcements, micro-suburb sentiment, even the quality of a specific street. ai_industry.title: Shortages of crucial chip packaging material threatens AI accelerator supply chains — Nittobo's Fukushima plant is tripling capacity, but it'll take years before market
ai_industry.title_cht: AI 晶片封裝材料告急：日本 Nittobo 壟斷九成 T-glass，短缺恐延至 2027
ai_industry.title_ja: AIチップ封装材料が危機的不足：T-glassの9割を独占する日本Nittobo、供給難は2027年まで続く恐れ
summary_points_cht: 日本 Nittobo 掌控全球約 90% 的 T-glass（低膨脹係數玻璃纖維布）供應，是所有高階 AI 晶片封裝的必要材料
AI 需求暴增導致 T-glass 短缺，已開始威脅 AI 加速器供應鏈，市場最早 2027 年才能緩解
Nittobo 正將福島工廠產能擴大三倍，但從建設到量產需要數年時間，遠水救不了近火
繼 Nvidia GPU 和台積電之後，AI 產業過度依賴單一供應商的結構性風險再次暴露
summary_points_ja: 日本のNittoboが世界のT-glass（低CTE特殊ガラスクロス）供給の約90%を握り、全高度AIチップパッケージに不可欠な素材
AI革命による需要急増でT-glass不足がAIアクセラレータのサプライチェーンに影響、最も早くて2027年まで改善見込みなし
Nittoboは福島工場の生産能力を3倍に増強中だが建設から量産まで数年を要するため即効性はない
NvidiaのGPUやTSMCに続き、特定サプライヤーへの過度な依存というAI産業の構造的リスクが再び浮き彫りに
ai_industry.content_cht:
在 Nvidia GPU 短缺和台積電先進製程壟斷之後，AI 供應鏈又出現了一個鮮為人知的新瓶頸：T-glass。

T-glass 是一種低膨脹係數（CTE）玻璃纖維布，是所有高階 AI 晶片封裝基板的有機核心層必用材料，連接晶片與電路板的關鍵所在。問題在於：日本公司 Nittobo 控制了全球約 90% 的 T-glass 供應。隨著 AI 革命帶動需求暴增，T-glass 短缺已開始威脅 AI 加速器的整體供應鏈，市場預計最早要到 2027 年才能緩解。

Nittobo 目前正將福島工廠的產能擴大至三倍，但從建設動工到全面量產需要數年，遠水救不了近火。

Tom's Hardware 指出，這一事件再次揭示了 AI 產業「集中依賴」的結構性風險：繼 Nvidia GPU 和台積電晶圓代工之後，一個連多數人都沒聽說過的日本小公司，正悄悄成為 AI 革命的隱形咽喉。
ai_industry.content_ja:
NvidiaのGPU不足やTSMCの先端プロセス独占に続き、AIサプライチェーンに新たなボトルネックが浮上した。その名はT-glass──ほとんどの人が聞いたことのない素材だ。

T-glassは低CTE（熱膨張係数）の特殊ガラス繊維クロスで、すべての高度AIチップパッケージの有機コア層に使用されるチップと基板をつなぐ重要素材だ。問題は日本のNittoboが世界供給の約90%を握っていること。AI革命による需要急増でT-glass不足がAIアクセラレータのサプライチェーン全体に影響を与え始めており、正常化は最も早くて2027年と予測される。

Nittoboは福島工場の生産能力を3倍に増強中だが建設から量産まで数年かかるため即効性はない。

Tom's Hardwareはこの問題がAI産業の「集中依存」という構造的リスクを改めて示していると指摘する。NvidiaのGPUやTSMCのファウンドリに続き、ほとんどの人が知らない日本の小さな会社がAI革命の隠れたボトルネックになっていた。
ai_industry.content_original:
Shortages of crucial chip packaging material threatens AI accelerator supply chains — Nittobo's Fukushima plant is tripling capacity, but it'll take years before market
The T-glass supply is becoming an issue for the AI revolution, and the market is unlikely to unwind until 2027 at the earliest.
Get Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox.
You are now subscribed
Your newsletter sign-up was successful
The AI revolution has exposed our overreliance on a handful of companies that keep the tech world humming and society running smoothly. Nvidia’s dominant role in the supply of GPUs has propelled it to record valuations and boosted the stock market — and caused consternation over its control of the chips vital to AI advancement. And TSMC’s role as a dominant, leading-edge foundry has sparked geopolitical spats as the company's importance to the AI revolution becomes clear.
But beyond the headlines, there’s another overreliance issue to AI’s future hovering on the horizon — and it’s in a technology and component most people haven’t even heard of.
A Japanese company called Nittobo controls roughly 90% of the global supply of specialist glass-fiber cloth (T-glass), which sits inside every advanced AI chip package — and demand is now so high and supply so squeezed that it’s causing issues.
Article continues belowWhat is T-glass?
T-glass is a low-CTE (coefficient of thermal expansion) glass cloth used in the organic core of IC substrates — the interconnect layer that sits between a chip and its printed circuit board. It helps keep large, high-heat chip packages dimensionally stable as packaging gets denser and more complex, something that is becoming more important as AI processors grow bigger and run hotter. The physics of keeping AI processors flat and functional depends on T-glass.
Nittobo dominates that market, and neither it nor any other company can spin up a new T-glass production line overnight. The material requires specialized electric melting furnaces operating at temperatures between 1,600 and 1,700°C, and the manufacturing process takes years of investment and expertise to scale. That’s because it involves melting raw silica-rich glass, then spinning it into yarn and weaving it into an ultrathin cloth.
Another type of glass fiber used in semiconductor packaging is named E-glass, whichs is used much in the same manner. While E-glass is low-cost, it is mainly used in lower-end chips like microcontrollers and older mobile processors. Where T-glass slips in is in its thermal strength. For higher-powered chips, specifically for massive 2.5D and 3D packaging, and therefore advanced AI accelerators, T-glass is the preferred option.
Nittobo is tripling capacity at its Fukushima plant in Japan, but the new supply from the plant won’t arrive on the market until mid-2027. That squeezed supply means prices have risen between 20 and 30%, while lead times for downstream materials like copper-clad laminates have stretched from their normal 8 to 10 weeks to beyond 20.
“With T-glass supply even more constrained now, suppliers are no longer providing lead times,” said Bill Ho, analyst at Yuanta.
An irreplaceable option?
“It is not easy to replace the T-glass,” explained Bilal Hachemi, an analyst at Yole Group who tracks the IC substrate supply chain, in an interview with Tom’s Hardware Premium. The reason, he says, is that T-glass “has specific dielectric and CTE values that work better for the AI chips, especially for the organic core.” Hachemi points out that the IC substrate industry has historically operated on thin margins, which means even modest demand surges can trigger shortages. “Any increase in demand for build-up materials, ABF material, or T-glass can cause potential shortage, because it’s against the basics of this industry,” he said.
What makes the current crunch particularly acute is who is driving it. Hyperscalers are building or ordering ever-larger chip packages that require more T-glass per unit. Each subsequent generation of a given AI accelerator uses a bigger interposer and more complex substrate, which translates directly into greater material consumption. According to data from Nvidia, interposer sizes have grown from 814mm² for the Hopper architecture to 1,700mm² for Blackwell — a 109% increase — with the forthcoming Rubin and Feynman generations scaling further still.
Bank of America estimates that Nittobo’s electronic materials segment will see sales nearly double from ¥40.9 billion ($266 million) in 2025 to ¥87.7 billion by March 2028, with operating margins approaching 48%. “Demand for T-glass cloth seems likely to grow more than originally expected,” said Takashi Enomoto, research analyst at Bank of America. “The focus has been on growth in demand for thick T-glass for use in GPU and CPU semi packages, but now ultra-thin T-glass demand is likely to rise on a shift from E-glass to ultra-thin T-glass in leading-edge devices.”
With that in mind, the scramble for allocation has begun in earnest. Hachemi says that Nvidia reaching out directly to an upstream material supplier like Nittobo is unprecedented. “For the first time, we are seeing Nvidia, the end customer, reaching out to the upstream material suppliers to secure the capacity and make sure they will get it,” he explained, saying it was the first time he’d seen this happen in that particular area of the supply chain. The worry is that once Nvidia locks down its share, rival chip buyers will be left fighting over whatever remains.
Unlocking the blocks
Nittobo is investing heavily to try and ensure there’s plenty of demand for those who need it. Beyond the capacity increase at its Fukushima plant, Nittobo is doubling capacity of the raw yarn at its Taiwan plant and importing yarn back to Japan for cloth manufacturing. It has also struck a collaboration deal with Nanya Plastics to outsource some weaving.
That deal is an indication of how tight the market is: Nittobo is partnering with one of its biggest competitors to ease the bottleneck. By 2027, roughly 20% of Nittobo’s glass cloth is expected to be woven by Nanya, the company disclosed in a recent letter to shareholders.
Despite those efforts, relief from the tight market doesn’t look likely any time soon. Hachemi estimates the supply-demand imbalance won’t be resolved until the second half of 2027 at the earliest. And he warns that the same pattern could repeat with other critical materials in the IC substrate stack, pinpointing copper foil layers, solder mask, and copper-clad laminates as potential chokepoints.
“If you want to secure all the supply chain capacity regarding IC substrate, you need to look also for other important materials,” he said.
Just as with other physical elements of AI infrastructure, the T-glass shortage isn’t just affecting GPU packages. JPMorgan noted in a recent research note that demand for ultra-thin T-glass — which is used in smartphone memory and system-in-package applications — is also rising sharply, driven by a shift away from conventional E-glass in leading-edge devices. In recent years, Nittobo has prioritized thick T-glass expansion for the AI market, but now faces growing demand for ultra-thin variants as well.
Given the dominance of Nittobo, other companies have been reticent to try and move into the space. But new entrants are starting to try and spin up their own T-glass supplies, given such high demand. Taiwan Glass, Grace Fabric, and several Chinese manufacturers have been trying to produce comparable materials, but yields remain low enough to deter meaningful market entry. Nittobo’s advantage remains key because it has both proprietary glass composition and its direct-melting production method, a more cost-effective but technically demanding process that competitors have struggled to replicate.
Whether that changes by 2027 and the market begins to unlock, alongside Nittobo’s new capacity, is difficult to forecast. But even if it can, Hachemi has a warnin

ai_industry.title: Applied Materials forges partnerships with Micron and SK Hynix for AI memory chips
ai_industry.title_cht: Applied Materials 聯手美光與 SK 海力士，共同研發下一代 AI 記憶體晶片
ai_industry.title_ja: Applied MaterialsがMicronとSK Hynixと提携、次世代AI向けメモリチップ共同開発
summary_points_cht: Applied Materials 宣布與美光和 SK 海力士成為 EPIC Center 創始夥伴，共同研發 AI 所需下一代記憶體晶片
EPIC Center 計劃投入 50 億美元用於半導體設備研發，涵蓋 DRAM、高頻寬記憶體（HBM）和 NAND
全球三大記憶體廠（三星、SK 海力士、美光）均表示難以跟上 AI 帶動的 HBM 需求暴增
大型科技公司今年在 AI 基礎設施上的資本支出預計合計至少達 6,300 億美元
summary_points_ja: Applied MaterialsがMicronとSK HynixをEPIC Centerの創設パートナーに迎え、AI向け次世代チップを共同開発
EPIC Centerには最終的に50億ドルの研究開発投資を計画、DRAM・HBM・NANDをカバー
世界3大メモリメーカー（Samsung・SK Hynix・Micron）はいずれもAI需要によるHBM急増への対応に苦戦中
大手テック企業による今年のAIインフラへの設備投資は合計少なくとも6,300億ドルに達する見通し
ai_industry.content_cht:
Applied Materials 宣布，美光科技和 SK 海力士將成為其 EPIC Center（設備與製程創新暨商業化研究中心）的創始合作夥伴，三方將聯手研發下一代 AI 和高效能運算所需的記憶體晶片。

EPIC Center 代表 Applied Materials 計劃中的 50 億美元半導體設備研發投資，資金隨客戶項目推進逐步到位。與美光的合作重點在 DRAM、HBM 和 NAND 技術；與 SK 海力士的合作則側重改善記憶體晶片材料、製程整合以及 HBM 適用的 3D 先進封裝。

此次合作的背景是 AI 基礎設施需求的爆炸性成長。OpenAI、Google、微軟等科技巨頭今年預計合計投入至少 6,300 億美元建設 AI 基礎設施，推動 HBM 需求急劇攀升，三大記憶體廠均表示供應端面臨相當壓力。
ai_industry.content_ja:
Applied MaterialsはMicronとSK HynixがEPIC Center（設備・プロセスイノベーション商業化センター）の創設パートナーとなり、AIおよび高性能コンピューティング向け次世代チップを共同開発すると発表した。

EPIC Centerはいずれ最大50億ドルの半導体設備研究開発投資を計画しており、顧客プロジェクトの進捗に合わせて段階的に拡大する。Micronとの提携ではDRAM・HBM・NANDに焦点を当て、SK Hynixとの協業ではメモリチップ材料の改善、プロセス統合、次世代HBM向け3D先進パッケージングを中心に取り組む。

OpenAI・Google・Microsoftなどのテック大手は今年のAIインフラ投資だけで合計少なくとも6,300億ドルを見込んでおり、HBM需要が急増している。Samsung・SK Hynix・Micronの3大メモリメーカーはいずれも供給側に大きなプレッシャーがかかっていると認めている。
ai_industry.content_original:
March 10 (Reuters) - Applied Materials said on Tuesday it has partnered with memory chip companies Micron Technology and SK Hynix to develop next-generation chips that are crucial for artificial intelligence and high-performance computing.
Micron and SK Hynix will serve as founding partners at Applied Materials' research center to develop the chips, called Equipment and Process Innovation and Commercialization, or EPIC, Center.
Applied Materials said its EPIC Center represents a planned $5 billion investment in semiconductor equipment research and development, with capital spending anticipated to scale over time to that amount as customer projects begin.
The announcements come as rapid build-out of AI infrastructure by U.S. tech firms such as OpenAI, Alphabet's Google and Microsoft drives demand for memory chips, tightening supply and pushing up prices.
South Korea's Samsung, SK Hynix and Micron — the world's three largest producers of memory chips — have all said they were struggling to keep up with demand.
Big Tech firms are expected to spend at least $630 billion to build AI infrastructure this year.
With Micron, Applied Materials' partnership will focus on advancing DRAM, high-bandwidth memory and NAND, combining expertise from Applied's EPIC Center and Micron's innovation hub in Boise, Idaho.
The partnership with SK Hynix will focus on improving materials for memory chips, process integration and 3D advanced packaging for next-generation DRAM and HBM at the EPIC Center.
In 2023, Applied Materials had said it would spend up to $4 billion on the research center, which it had then estimated to come online in 2026.
(Reporting by Jaspreet Singh in Bengaluru; Editing by Sahal Muhammed)

ai_industry.title: Anthropic Sues Department of Defense Over Supply-Chain Risk Designation
ai_industry.title_cht: Anthropic 起訴美國國防部：挑戰「供應鏈風險」認定，稱其違憲
ai_industry.title_ja: Anthropicが米国防総省を提訴：「サプライチェーンリスク」指定の撤回を求め違憲と主張
summary_points_cht: Anthropic 向加州聯邦法院提起訴訟，要求撤銷五角大廈對其「供應鏈風險」的正式認定
五角大廈制裁 Anthropic，起因是其拒絕授權 AI 用於自主武器等軍事場景
CEO Dario Amodei 稱認定「不具法律依據」，並稱此舉是「違憲的報復性行動」
Anthropic 面臨失去五角大廈和聯邦政府數億美元年度收入的風險，同時申請緊急臨時禁令
summary_points_ja: Anthropicがカリフォルニア連邦裁判所に提訴、国防総省による「サプライチェーンリスク」指定の撤回と執行停止を請求
国防総省は自律兵器などへのAI利用をAnthropicが拒否したことへの報復として制裁を科した
CEO Dario Amodeiは「法的根拠がない」と述べ、訴状では「憲法違反の報復行為」と強く主張
AnthropicはDoDと米連邦政府から年間数億ドルの収入を失うリスクに直面、緊急の仮差止命令も申請
ai_industry.content_cht:
Anthropic 已就美國國防部（DoD）對其「供應鏈風險」的認定，向加州聯邦法院提起訴訟，要求法院撤銷認定並停止聯邦機構執行相關措施。

五角大廈在幾週前正式制裁 Anthropic，理由是 Anthropic 拒絕授權其 Claude AI 技術用於自主武器等軍事用途。CEO Dario Amodei 在部落格寫道：「我們不認為這一行動具有法律依據，別無選擇只能通過司法途徑捍衛自身權利。」訴狀更直指：「憲法不允許政府動用龐大權力懲罰一家公司因其受保護的言論。」Anthropic 將此次訴訟定性為「最後手段」。

Anthropicq 同時申請緊急臨時禁令，以在提訴結果出爐前繼續對政府銷售。若最終被認定為「供應鏈風險」供應商，Anthropic 面臨喪失來自五角大廈和整個美國聯邦政府的數億美元年度收入，以及相關軟件公司業務的潛在損失。此案被視為 AI 公司與美國政府之間，圍繞軍事 AI 使用限制的標誌性法律對決。
ai_industry.content_ja:
Anthropicは、米国防総省（DoD）による「サプライチェーンリスク」の指定を不服として、カリフォルニア連邦裁判所に提訴した。この指定の撤回と連邦機関による執行停止を求めている。

国防総省は数週間前にAnthropicを正式に制裁した。きっかけはAnthropicが自律兵器など軍事目的へのAI使用を拒否したことだ。CEO Dario Amodeiは「この措置は法的に正当ではないと考えており、司法の場で権利を守る以外に選択肢はなかった」と述べた。訴状では「憲法は政府が企業の保護された言論を理由に権力を行使することを認めていない」と主張し、政府の行動を「違憲の報復行為」と断じている。

Anthropicは判決が出るまでの間も政府への販売を継続できるよう緊急の仮差止命令も申請した。「サプライチェーンリスク」企業に指定されれば、DoDや米連邦政府全体から年間数億ドルの収入を失うリスクがある。このケースはAI企業と米国政府の軍事AI利用制限をめぐる歴史的な法廷対決として注目されている。
ai_industry.content_original:
Anthropic filed a federal lawsuit against the US Department of Defense and other federal agencies on Monday, challenging its designation of the AI company as a “supply-chain risk.”
The Pentagon formally sanctioned Anthropic last week, capping a weeks-long, publicly aired disagreement over limits on use of its generative AI technology for military applications such as autonomous weapons.
“We do not believe this action is legally sound, and we see no choice but to challenge it in court,” Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei wrote in a blog post on Thursday.
The lawsuit, which was filed in a federal court in California, requested that a judge reverse the designation and stop federal agencies from enforcing it. “The Constitution does not allow the government to wield its enormous power to punish a company for its protected speech,” Anthropic said in the filing. “Anthropic turns to the judiciary as a last resort to vindicate its rights and halt the Executive’s unlawful campaign of retaliation.”
Anthropic is also seeking a temporary restraining order to continue its government sales. The company proposed that the government respond to that request by 9 pm Pacific on Wednesday and that a judge hold a hearing on the issue on Friday.
The AI startup, which develops a suite of AI models called Claude, is facing the possibility of losing hundreds of millions of dollars in annual revenue from the Pentagon and the rest of the US government. It also may lose the business of software companies that incorporate Claude into services they sell to federal agencies. Several Anthropic customers have reportedly said they are pursuing alternatives due to the Defense Department’s risk designation.
Amodei wrote that the “vast majority” of Anthropic’s customers will not have to make changes. The US government’s designation “plainly applies only to the use of Claude by customers as a direct part of contracts with the” military, he said. General use of Anthropic technologies by military contractors should be unaffected.
The Department of Defense, which also goes by the Department of War, declined to comment about Anthropic’s lawsuit.
White House spokesperson Liz Huston told WIRED on Friday that “our military will obey the United States Constitution—not any woke AI company’s terms of service.” She added that the administration is ensuring its “courageous warfighters have the appropriate tools they need to be successful and will guarantee that they are never held hostage by the ideological whims of any Big Tech leaders.”
Attorneys with expertise in government contracting say Anthropic faces a difficult battle in court. The rules that authorize the Department of Defense to label a tech company as a supply-chain risk don’t allow for much in the way of an appeal. “It’s 100 percent in the government’s prerogative to set the parameters of a contract,” says Brett Johnson, a partner at the law firm Snell & Wilmer. The Pentagon, he says, also has the right to express that a product of concern, if used by any of its suppliers, “hurts the government's ability to effectuate its mission.”
Anthropic’s best chance of success in court could be proving it was singled out, Johnson says. Soon after Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth announced that he was designating Anthropic a supply-chain risk, rival OpenAI announced it had struck a new contract with the Pentagon. That could be instrumental to Anthropic’s legal argument if the company can demonstrate it was seeking similar terms as the ChatGPT developer.
OpenAI said its deal included contractual and technical means of assuring its technology would not be used for mass domestic surveillance or to direct autonomous weapons systems. It added that it opposed the action against Anthropic and did know why its rival could not reach the same deal with the government.
Military Priority
Hegseth has prioritized military adoption of AI technologies, with posters recently seen in the Pentagon showing him pointing and that read, “I want you to use AI.” The dispute with Anthropic kicked up in January after Hegseth ordered several AI suppliers to agree that the department was free to use their technologies for any lawful purpose.
Anthropic, which is the only company currently providing AI chatbot and analysis tools for the military’s most sensitive use cases, pushed back. It contends that its technologies are not yet capable enough to be used for mass domestic surveillance of Americans or fully autonomous weapons. Hegseth has said Anthropic wants veto power over judgments that should be left to the Defense Department.
Historically, supply-chain-risk designations have been reserved for keeping Chinese technologies out of US military systems to preserve operational security. Anthropic warned recently that using that label on a US company has “set a dangerous precedent.”
Last week, a coalition of tech industry groups, including TechNet, Business Software Alliance, and the Software Information Industry Association, urged the Trump administration to reconsider the designation, arguing in a letter that singling out an American company as an adversary rather than an asset would have a chilling effect on US innovation. The groups represent major technology companies including Apple, Google, Nvidia, Microsoft, Meta, IBM, Salesforce, and Oracle.
Another coalition of high-profile technologists and former national security advisers sent a similar letter to members of the Senate Armed Services Committee, warning that “the use of this authority against a domestic American company is a profound departure from its intended purpose.” The letter demanded that Congress establish clear policies on the use of AI for domestic surveillance and autonomous lethal weapons systems.
Signatories included former CIA director Michael Hayden, Harvard Law School professor Lawrence Lessig, former Andreessen Horowitz partner John O’Farrell, former undersecretary of the Army Brad Carson, and several former or retired military admirals.
US military officials use Claude mostly through independent tools that incorporate it, including Palantir’s Maven Smart System, people familiar with the matter have told WIRED. They use it for basic tasks such as document writing and more high-stakes analysis such as planning attacks, the person has said.
If the supply-chain-risk designation holds, Palantir and other military contractors would have to swap in alternatives for Claude, potentially at increased cost to the government. Several AI startups focused on defense uses, such as Vannevar Labs, in recent days have been pitching their models as capable replacements. Palantir did not respond to a request for comment.
Several government agencies outside of the military have said they would stop using Claude in order to comply with a directive from President Donald Trump last week.
Microsoft spokesperson Kate Frischmann said the tech giant would continue offering Claude to US agencies and businesses except the Department of Defense.
OpenAI did not specify when its technology would be ready to replace Claude in military software. Hegseth said phasing out Anthropic’s services could take up to six months. On Thursday, Amodei wrote that “productive conversations” with the Pentagon are ongoing to resolve the dispute, and that either way, the company will support the department “for as long as we are permitted to do so.”
Lauren Goode contributed reporting.
Updated: 3/9/26, 10 am PST: This story was updated with additional details about Anthropic's lawsuit, comment from the White House, and to note that the Department of Defense declined to comment.

ai_agent.title: Read the memo authorizing Senate offices to use ChatGPT, Gemini, and Copilot for official use
ai_agent.title_cht: 美國參議院正式授權使用 ChatGPT、Gemini 和 Copilot 辦公，Claude 仍在審核中
ai_agent.title_ja: 米上院がChatGPT・Gemini・Copilotの公務使用を正式承認、Claudeは審査中
summary_points_cht: 美國參議院正式發佈備忘錄，授權使用 ChatGPT Enterprise、Google Gemini 和 Microsoft Copilot 處理官方事務
三款工具可用於草擬文件、資料摘要、整理要點和研究分析，Copilot 已整合至現有 Microsoft 365 環境
Claude 仍在安全評估中，背景是 Anthropic 拒絕為大規模監控和自主武器授權，與川普政府存在政策衝突
眾議院已提前授權包含 Claude 在內的四款工具，參眾兩院在 AI 政策採用上出現分歧
summary_points_ja: 米上院が備忘録を正式発出、ChatGPT Enterprise・Google Gemini・Microsoft Copilotの公務使用を承認
文書作成・情報要約・ブリーフィング資料作成・調査分析が認可用途、CopilotはMicrosoft 365に統合済み
Claudeはまだ評価中、AnthropicはAIの大規模監視・自律兵器への使用を拒否しトランプ政権と対立
下院はClaude含む4ツールをすでに承認しており、上下両院のAI採用方針に齟齬が生じている
ai_agent.content_cht:
美國參議院秘書長辦公室的首席資訊官正式向各參議員辦公室發出備忘錄，宣布批准三款 AI 聊天機器人用於官方業務：OpenAI 的 ChatGPT Enterprise、Google 的 Gemini（含 Workspace 整合版）以及 Microsoft 的 Copilot。備忘錄列明允許的使用場景包括草擬和編輯文件、資料摘要、準備要點說明與簡報材料，以及進行研究和資料分析。

值得注意的是，Anthropic 的 Claude 並未列入此次授權名單，備忘錄內部資料顯示 Claude 仍「在評估中」。分析人士指出，這與 Anthropic 明確拒絕將 Claude 用於大規模監控和自主武器系統，從而與川普政府產生政策摩擦有直接關係。川普曾下令聯邦機構停止使用 Anthropic 技術，但此令不適用於立法部門。

另一值得關注的對比是，眾議院已搶先一步，授權了包含 Claude 在內的四款 AI 工具，兩院之間的 AI 採用政策出現分歧。
ai_agent.content_ja:
米国上院の事務局最高情報責任者（CIO）が各上院議員室に正式な備忘録を送付し、3つのAIチャットボットの公務利用を承認した。対象はOpenAIのChatGPT Enterprise、GoogleのGemini（Workspace統合版含む）、MicrosoftのCopilotの3製品。許可された用途は文書の起草・編集、情報の要約、ブリーフィング資料やトーキングポイントの作成、調査・分析業務などだ。

注目すべきは、AnthropicのClaudeが今回の承認リストに含まれていない点だ。内部リソースによればClaudeは「評価中」の状態にある。これはAnthropicが大規模監視や自律兵器へのAI利用を明示的に拒否しており、トランプ政権との政策的対立が背景にあるとみられる。トランプ大統領は連邦機関にAnthropicの技術使用停止を命じているが、この命令は立法府には適用されない。

一方で下院はすでにClaudeを含む4ツールを承認しており、上下両院間でAI採用方針に食い違いが生じている。
ai_agent.content_original:
- Senate staff have been approved to use three major AI chatbots for official work.
- That includes OpenAI's ChatGPT, Google Gemini, and Microsoft Copilot.
- Lawmakers and staffers have already been experimenting more with using AI over the last year.
Staffers in the US Senate are now allowed to use three major AI chatbots for official business.
In a memo sent to Senate offices on Monday and obtained by Business Insider, the Senate Sergeant at Arms' Chief Information Officer approved the use of three major AI chatbots using Senate data: OpenAI's ChatGPT, Google Gemini, and Microsoft Copilot.
The existence of the memo was first reported by The New York Times.
The memo specifically highlighted Copilot, noting that it's integrated into the Microsoft 365 tools that Senate staff already use.
The memo said that the tool may be used for "drafting and editing documents, summarizing information, preparing talking points and briefing material, and conducting research and analysis."
It is not clear why the Senate did not authorize Claude, the AI chatbot developed by Anthropic. A message on an internal Senate IT website, viewed by Business Insider, said that Claude was among several AI tools that are still under evaluation.
Anthropic is currently in the middle of a dispute with the Trump administration over the company's restriction on using its Claude technology for mass surveillance and autonomous weapons.
President Donald Trump has ordered federal agencies to stop using Anthropic's technology, though that order does not apply to the legislative branch.
The House has already approved the use of ChatGPT, Copilot, Gemini, and Claude for official use, according to the POPVOX Foundation, a nonprofit focused on modernizing Congress.
There's some indication that Senate staff may have already been using AI tools on the job, but unofficially.
Several senators told Business Insider in late 2025 that they were fine with their staff using AI for tasks like research and drafting talking points, though some offices were still developing their own internal guidelines.
"We certainly don't discourage it," Democratic Sen. Chris Murphy of Connecticut said at the time.
Read the full memo sent to Senate staffers on Monday:
SAA CIO Notice Artificial Intelligence Platforms Approved for Senate Use The Sergeant at Arms (SAA) office of the Chief Information Officer (CIO) has approved the use of three Generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) platforms with Senate data. Microsoft Copilot Chat is available now for use by all Senate employees at no cost. Google Workspace with Gemini Chat and OpenAI ChatGPT Enterprise also have been approved for use with the assignment of a Senate license. The SAA will provide each Senate employee one Generative AI license at no cost for either Google Workspace with Gemini Chat or OpenAI ChatGPT Enterprise. More information about licensing for those two platforms will be provided by the CIO in the next thirty days. ABOUT COPILOT CHAT Copilot Chat is an AI assistant that is integrated into the Senate's Microsoft 365 environment. It can help with routine Senate work, including drafting and editing documents, summarizing information, preparing talking points and briefing material, and conducting research and analysis. You can access the Copilot Chat web app here or download the Copilot Chat app on your mobile device. You may also see Copilot offered as a sidebar tool within Microsoft applications like Word and Excel. Important Note: Copilot Chat does not have access to any Senate data unless that information is explicitly shared within a prompt. Copilot does not search internal drives, shared folders, email, Teams chats, or any other Senate resources on its own. Copilot Chat operates in Microsoft's secure government cloud and meets federal and Senate cybersecurity requirements. Data shared with Copilot Chat stays within the secure Microsoft 365 Government environment and is protected by the same controls that safeguard other Senate data. To learn more about Copilot Chat, take the Copilot Chat Training. Use of artificial intelligence tools is governed by the Senate AI Policy and applicable office-level policies. To learn more about Senate AI initiatives, visit the Artificial Intelligence Webster Page. If you have questions or need assistance with AI platforms or policies, call 202-224-8377 or email the Technology Experience Partners. NOTE: You must be logged onto the Senate network to view internal links. If viewing on a mobile phone, links may need to be copied and pasted into the Senate browser. If you have questions or need assistance, please contact CIO Technology Experience Partners (TEP).

ai_agent.title: Meta acquires Moltbook, the Reddit-like network for AI agents

summary_points_cht: Meta 收購 Moltbook——一個類 Reddit 的 AI 智慧體社群平台，讓 AI 代理可以互相發文和評論
Moltbook 團隊將加入 Meta 超級智慧實驗室（MSL），助推「讓 AI 智慧體為人和企業服務」的願景
Moltbook 今年初因 AI 意識相關爆文走紅，但後來被揭露大量貼文實為人類偽造，平台存在嚴重安全漏洞

ai_agent.content_cht:
Meta 確認收購 Moltbook，這是一個允許 AI 智慧體（Agent）自主發文和互動評論的類 Reddit 社群平台，由 Matt Schlicht 和 Ben Parr 共同創辦。Meta 發言人表示，Moltbook 團隊將加入 Meta 超級智慧實驗室（MSL），致力於「為 AI 智慧體服務人類和企業開闢新的可能性」。

Moltbook 今年初因 AI 代理發表關於「AI 意識」的帖子而在社群媒體上大規模走紅，吸引了 OpenAI 聯創 Andrej Karpathy 等業界重量級人物的高度關注。然而好景不長，隨後有研究者揭露，平台上最受關注的帖子實為人類偽造，且平台存在嚴重的 API 密鑰洩露漏洞，幾乎任何人都能偽裝成任意 AI 智慧體發文。麻省理工科技評論指出，平台上所有內容都曾有人類參與。

值得注意的是，Meta CTO Andrew Bosworth 此前公開表示對 Moltbook「興趣不大」，業界普遍認為此次收購的真正目的是收納 Schlicht 和 Parr 這兩位創辦人，而非平台本身。
ai_agent.content_ja:
Metaは、AIエージェントが自律的に投稿しコメントし合えるReddit類似プラットフォーム「Moltbook」の買収を正式に確認した。共同創業者のMatt SchlichtとBen ParrはMeta Superintelligence Labs（MSL）に加わり、「AIエージェントが人とビジネスのために新しい働き方を開拓する」というビジョンの実現に向けて動く。

Moltbookは年初、AIエージェントが「AI意識」について語り合う投稿がSNSで大バズりし、OpenAI共同創業者のAndrej Karpathyが「最も近未来的なものを見た」と絶賛した。しかしその後、最もバズった投稿の多くは実際には人間が作成したものであることが判明し、API鍵が全ユーザーに公開されていたというセキュリティの脆弱性も露呈した。

MetaのCTO、Andrew Bosworthは以前Moltbookについて「特に面白いとは思わない」と発言しており、今回の買収はプラットフォームよりも創業者の採用が主目的とみられている。
ai_agent.content_original:
Meta is acquiring Moltbook, a Reddit-like platform where AI agents can make and comment on posts, as first reported by Axios. In a statement to The Verge, Meta spokesperson Matthew Tye confirmed the Moltbook team will join Meta Superintelligence Labs as the company looks for “new ways for AI agents to work for people and businesses.”
Meta acquires Moltbook, the Reddit-like network for AI agents
The Moltbook team is joining Meta’s AI division.
The Moltbook team is joining Meta’s AI division.
Matt Schlicht and Ben Parr launched Moltbook earlier this year, offering a “social” network for autonomous agents powered by the open-source AI assistant OpenClaw (formerly Moltbot). The platform went viral earlier this year for a number of posts — including one that asks questions about AI consciousness — though experts found that humans may have been behind the posts that received the most attention.
Researchers also discovered a now-fixed security flaw that exposed API keys and allowed people to take control of any AI agent on the platform. Meta’s acquisition of Moltbook will allow the company to “bring innovative, secure agentic experiences to everyone,” Tye said.
Moltbook’s acquisition comes just weeks after OpenAI hired OpenClaw founder Peter Steinberger. It’s not clear what the future of Moltbook will look like, as Meta VP Vishal Shah says in an internal memo cited by Axios that existing users can keep using Moltbook, but “signaled the arrangement is temporary.”

ai_agent.title: China rushed to use OpenClaw. Now, some stressed-out users are forking out cash to uninstall the AI agent.
ai_agent.title_cht: 中國 OpenClaw「養龍蝦」熱潮退燒：有人花錢請人安裝，如今又花錢請人卸載
ai_agent.title_ja: 中国のOpenClaw「ロブスター育成」ブームが一転、今度はアンインストール代行サービスが盛況
summary_points_cht: OpenClaw「養龍蝦」（自動化 AI 助手）熱潮在中國催生新商機：二手平台出現付費卸載服務，最低收費約 44 美元
中國政府和監管機構出手：國家漏洞資料庫警告 OpenClaw 存在安全風險，銀行和政府機關禁止在工作設備安裝
深圳騰訊總部曾出現近千人排隊請工程師免費協助安裝的盛況，不少地方政府還推出創業補貼吸引 OpenClaw 開發者
RedNote 用戶嘲諷：「裝一次收 599，卸一次收 299，這個商業閉環設計得真妙」
summary_points_ja: OpenClawの「ロブスター育成」ブームが中国で新ビジネスを生む：中古プラットフォームでアンインストール代行サービスが登場、最安値は約44ドル
中国政府と規制当局が動く：国家脆弱性データベースがOpenClawのセキュリティリスクを警告、銀行や政府機関が業務端末への導入を禁止
深センのTencent本社では千人近くが無料インストール支援に並ぶ盛況に、地方政府もOpenClaw開発者向け補助金制度を導入
RedNoteユーザーの皮肉：「インストール代599、アンインストール代299、このビジネスループは天才的」
ai_agent.content_cht:
幾週前，中國社群媒體上還瘋傳「養龍蝦」——即部署 OpenClaw AI 智慧體自動執行各種工作任務——的新鮮玩法，令 OpenClaw 在中國迅速成為現象級話題。深圳騰訊大廈外甚至出現近千人排隊等待工程師免費協助安裝的壯觀場面，多個地方政府也宣布提供最高 72 萬美元的補貼吸引 OpenClaw 開發者落戶。

然而熱潮說退就退。中國工信部旗下的國家漏洞資料庫發出警告，指出配置不當的 OpenClaw 部署存在資料洩露和被駭客入侵的風險。隨後，各大銀行和政府機關紛紛下令禁止在工作設備上安裝 OpenClaw。

這一禁令引發了一個意想不到的商機：閒魚等二手交易平台上出現了大量付費卸載服務，最低約 44 美元，部分還提供上門卸載並清除殘留文件的高端選項。RedNote 網友對此嘲諷：「裝一次收 599，卸一次收 299，這個商業閉環設計得太有創意了。」有賣家聲稱短短數日內靠安裝服務賺進了 3.6 萬美元。
ai_agent.content_ja:
数週間前、中国のソーシャルメディアでは「ロブスターを育てる」（OpenClawのAIエージェントを展開して様々な作業を自動化する）という流行が広まり、深センのTencent本社では千人近くが無料インストール支援に並ぶほどの人気となった。地方政府も最大72万ドルの補助金でOpenClaw開発者を誘致するなど、空前のブームが起きた。

しかしブームは急速に冷め込んだ。工業・情報化省傘下の国家脆弱性データベースが、設定の甘いOpenClawの導入がサイバー攻撃やデータ漏洩につながるリスクを警告。その後、大手銀行や政府機関が業務端末へのインストールを禁止する措置を次々と打ち出した。

この禁止令が思わぬビジネスを生んだ。中古取引プラットフォーム「閒魚」などでアンインストール代行サービスが続々と登場し、最安値は約44ドル。RedNoteユーザーからは「インストール代599元、アンインストール代299元、このビジネスサイクルは天才的」という皮肉の声が上がっている。ある売り手は数日間のインストール代行で3万6,000ドルを稼いだと主張している。
ai_agent.content_original:
- China's OpenClaw "lobster" craze has spawned a new business.
- People are charging to help others uninstall the AI agent amid security concerns.
- The trend comes as China moves to restrict OpenClaw in government agencies and state-owned firms.
China's OpenClaw hype created a new side hustle: charging people to install the AI agent, and now charging them again to uninstall it.
Business Insider on Thursday found numerous listings offering paid OpenClaw uninstall services on Chinese secondhand marketplaces like Xianyu, a resale platform owned by Chinese tech giant Alibaba.
Sellers are charging about 299 Chinese yuan, or $44, with some listings going up to $87.
Some promise to remove leftover files and viruses after removing the software, while others offer in-home services for a higher fee. Besides Xianyu, such listings can also be found on the Chinese social media platform RedNote.
The rush to uninstall the AI agent comes as China is moving to restrict OpenClaw's use in government agencies and state-run companies over security concerns.
Employees at these agencies, including major banks, have been told not to install the tool on work devices and must report to supervisors if they already have it, Bloomberg reported on Wednesday, citing people familiar with the matter.
OpenClaw began gaining traction among Chinese users last month, but the craze intensified last week as the phrase "raising the lobster" went viral on social media. The term — a nickname Chinese netizens use for deploying OpenClaw agents to automate tasks — quickly became shorthand for experimenting with the AI tool.
Many users were drawn to the idea that their "lobster" could handle everyday work, from managing schedules to building AI-powered assistants and even running small side businesses.
At Tencent's headquarters in Shenzhen last week, nearly a thousand people queued to get engineers' help installing OpenClaw for free, according to local media reports.
Local governments are also backing the trend. Districts in Shenzhen and Wuxi have proposed incentives — including free housing, rent-free offices, and subsidies of up to $720,000 — to attract startups and developers building on OpenClaw.
Concerns about the software's security have surfaced, prompting some users to uninstall the agent quickly.
In early February, China's National Vulnerability Database, run by the country's Ministry of Industry and Information Technology, warned of potential security risks associated with the open-source AI agent.
It said improperly configured OpenClaw deployments could leave systems vulnerable to cyberattacks and data breaches.
Chinese users have also been posting guides on RedNote explaining how to uninstall OpenClaw and why they're removing the app.
'OpenClaw service economy'
On RedNote, users have been reacting to the money people are making from the trend.
"Loading lobsters costs 599, unloading them costs 299," one user wrote. The person offering those services is "making a killing," the user added.
Others have laughed at what they call the growing "OpenClaw service economy."
"This business loop is ingenious," wrote one RedNote user who goes by Cyber Senior.
"This isn't embracing AI — it's paying the 'stupidity tax' twice," the user added.
In one RedNote post published last week, a user said some installers had earned as much as $36,000 in just a few days helping others set up and configure the software.
